CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from methodology (encoder-decoder, attention, copy mechanism) to experiments (multiple datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics) and conclusions. The main contributions (Deep Keyphrase Generation model, CopyRNN variant, and the large KP20k corpus) are stated early and repeatedly, and related work and hyperparameters are thoroughly reported. Notation and presentation seem consistent and examples/tables (e.g., error examples, comparative results) support understanding, though the writing is described only as "adequate," suggesting minor clarity or stylistic issues remain rather than major structural problems. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from model description (encoder–decoder, attention, copy mechanism) to experiments (multiple datasets and baselines) and conclusion, and the main contributions (Deep Keyphrase Generation, CopyRNN, large training/test corpora) are stated early. Notation and methodological description seem consistent and sufficiently defined for reproducing the approach (bidirectional GRU encoder, GRU decoder, beam search) and hyperparameters are reported. Figures/tables and examples appear informative—e.g., performance comparisons across many datasets and concrete mistaken-prediction examples—while related work coverage is thorough. Writing quality is adequate overall, with only minor presentation issues implied rather than prominent.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem (keyphrase generation) and introduces a concrete generative solution (CopyRNN with attention and copying) that demonstrably outperforms extraction baselines (over 20% average improvement on present keyphrases) and can generate absent topical keyphrases. Its use of a very large scientific training corpus (527,830 articles) and the new KP20k test set materially strengthens empirical claims and provides a useful resource for follow-up work. This approach is likely to change how researchers approach keyphrase extraction (shifting toward generative/copying models), open new research directions (absent phrase generation, domain adaptation), and be widely built upon in related NLP tasks. | B(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—automatic keyphrase generation—and introduces a generative encoder–decoder (CopyRNN) with attention and a copy mechanism that substantially outperforms traditional extractive baselines (over 20% improvement on present keyphrases) and can generate absent (not-in-text) topical keyphrases. The construction of a very large training corpus (567,830 articles) and a new 20,000-item test set (KP20k) increases its empirical significance and reproducibility. This approach is likely to change how researchers approach keyphrase extraction (shifting from purely extractive methods to generative models), open new directions (handling absent phrase generation, copy mechanisms, domain adaptation), and be widely built upon, though its impact is somewhat tempered by vocabulary/OOV and domain-transfer limitations.

The construction of a very large training corpus (567,830 articles) and a new 20,000-item test set (KP20k) increases its empirical significance and reproducibility. This approach is likely to change how researchers approach keyphrase extraction (shifting from purely extractive methods to generative models), open new directions (handling absent phrase generation, copy mechanisms, domain adaptation), and be widely built upon, though its impact is somewhat tempered by vocabulary/OOV and domain-transfer limitations.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This is squarely within a top NLP/ML venue: it proposes a neural encoder–decoder model (CopyRNN with attention and copy mechanism) for a core NLP task (keyphrase generation), introduces a large new dataset (KP20k and a 567k-article training corpus), and compares against standard baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui) using accepted metrics (precision/recall/F1). The methodology (bidirectional GRU encoder, attention, copy mechanism, beam search) and evaluation protocol match expectations for the venue, and the topic addresses the right community interested in generative sequence models and information extraction. | B(5): This work fits squarely within top NLP/ML venues: it addresses keyphrase generation using modern seq2seq techniques (bidirectional GRU encoder, attention, copy mechanism) and targets the NLP audience. The methodology and evaluation meet community standards—CopyRNN is compared against established baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui) on multiple datasets and a newly introduced large test set (KP20k) using standard precision/recall/F1 metrics. The dataset scale, clear problem formulation, and positioning within related work make it a strong fit for a top-tier conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This is squarely within a top NLP/ML venue: it proposes a neural encoder–decoder model (CopyRNN with attention and copy mechanism) for a core NLP task (keyphrase generation), introduces a large new dataset (KP20k and a 567k-article training corpus), and compares against standard baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui) using accepted metrics (precision/recall/F1). The methodology (bidirectional GRU encoder, attention, copy mechanism, beam search) and evaluation protocol match expectations for the venue, and the topic addresses the right community interested in generative sequence models and information extraction.

The dataset scale, clear problem formulation, and positioning within related work make it a strong fit for a top-tier conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents comprehensive experiments across multiple standard datasets (Krapivin, Inspec, NUS, SemEval-2010, KP20k) and a large collected corpus, and compares to a range of baselines with standard metrics, supporting the main claims. It includes an ablation-like comparison between a vanilla RNN and the CopyRNN variant showing the copy mechanism's benefit, and reports hyperparameters. Related work is described as thorough and several failure modes and limitations (OOV issues, erroneous generations, ExpandRank scaling, cross-domain mismatch) are discussed. Reproducibility is partially supported (dataset sizes and hyperparameters reported) but code is not mentioned, so full replication may be hindered. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is strong: the model is trained on a very large corpus (527,830 train articles), tested on multiple standard datasets plus a new large KP20k split, and compared against a wide set of classical baselines, supporting the main claims about CopyRNN and its ability to generate absent keyphrases. Some ablation/sensitivity analysis is limited — they do contrast CopyRNN with a vanilla RNN (an important ablation) but do not appear to report deeper sensitivity studies (e.g., attention vs. no-attention, vocabulary cutoff, beam-size effects). Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several failure cases/limitations are discussed (vocabulary OOV issues, mistaken predictions, domain mismatch), while hyperparameters are reported which aids reproducibility (though code availability is not mentioned). Overall the paper has solid experimental breadth with minor gaps in fine-grained analyses.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents comprehensive experiments across multiple standard datasets (Krapivin, Inspec, NUS, SemEval-2010, KP20k) and a large collected corpus, and compares to a range of baselines with standard metrics, supporting the main claims. It includes an ablation-like comparison between a vanilla RNN and the CopyRNN variant showing the copy mechanism's benefit, and reports hyperparameters. Related work is described as thorough and several failure modes and limitations (OOV issues, erroneous generations, ExpandRank scaling, cross-domain mismatch) are discussed. Reproducibility is partially supported (dataset sizes and hyperparameters reported) but code is not mentioned, so full replication may be hindered.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong: the model is trained on a very large corpus (527,830 train articles), tested on multiple standard datasets plus a new large KP20k split, and compared against a wide set of classical baselines, supporting the main claims about CopyRNN and its ability to generate absent keyphrases. Some ablation/sensitivity analysis is limited — they do contrast CopyRNN with a vanilla RNN (an important ablation) but do not appear to report deeper sensitivity studies (e.g., attention vs. no-attention, vocabulary cutoff, beam-size effects). Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several failure cases/limitations are discussed (vocabulary OOV issues, mistaken predictions, domain mismatch), while hyperparameters are reported which aids reproducibility (though code availability is not mentioned). Overall the paper has solid experimental breadth with minor gaps in fine-grained analyses.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable novel framing for keyphrase generation by using an encoder-decoder generative model (Deep Keyphrase Generation) and a CopyRNN variant that incorporates attention and a copy mechanism to handle OOVs and produce absent keyphrases—a clear departure from prior extraction-only baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, etc.). It also contributes a substantial training corpus and a new large test set (KP20k), which strengthens the originality of the work. While the underlying components (RNN encoder-decoder, attention, copy mechanisms) are established in other NLP tasks, their application and joint design for keyphrase generation represent a non-trivial and well-differentiated extension rather than a purely incremental combination. | B(4): The paper presents a notable novel perspective by casting keyphrase prediction as a generative encoder–decoder task and introducing CopyRNN (attention + copy mechanism) to both extract present phrases and generate absent keyphrases — a capability prior extractive methods lacked. It also contributes a large training corpus and the KP20k test set, strengthening the empirical novelty. The technical approach largely composes existing seq2seq, attention, and copying mechanisms, so the methods are a non-trivial but expected extension rather than a fundamentally new algorithm. Overall the work is a clear and informative advance in the keyphrase literature, though built from existing components.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable novel framing for keyphrase generation by using an encoder-decoder generative model (Deep Keyphrase Generation) and a CopyRNN variant that incorporates attention and a copy mechanism to handle OOVs and produce absent keyphrases—a clear departure from prior extraction-only baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, etc.). It also contributes a substantial training corpus and a new large test set (KP20k), which strengthens the originality of the work. While the underlying components (RNN encoder-decoder, attention, copy mechanisms) are established in other NLP tasks, their application and joint design for keyphrase generation represent a non-trivial and well-differentiated extension rather than a purely incremental combination.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are plausibly supported: it introduces a standard encoder–decoder with attention plus a copy mechanism (CopyRNN), trains on a large corpus (527,830 train articles) and evaluates on multiple datasets including a new KP20k testset, reporting substantial (>20%) gains on present-keyphrase extraction. The model design and training objective are technically sound and consistent with established sequence-to-sequence and copy mechanisms. Minor methodological gaps remain — comparisons are against traditional extractive baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui) with no mention of modern neural baselines or statistical significance testing — but these do not materially undermine the reported empirical improvements. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented experiments, with only small shortcomings in experimental breadth and reporting. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly supported by extensive experiments: a generative encoder-decoder (bi-GRU encoder, GRU decoder) with attention and a copy mechanism (CopyRNN) is evaluated on multiple standard datasets and a large new KP20k test set, showing >20% improvement over strong baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, Maui, etc.) on present keyphrase extraction. The methodological description (model architecture, training on a 527k+ corpus, beam search inference) and reported metrics (precision/recall/F1) make the experimental results credible. Minor technical concerns remain — no mention of statistical significance testing or code release for reproducibility is given — but these do not undermine the main experimental claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are plausibly supported: it introduces a standard encoder–decoder with attention plus a copy mechanism (CopyRNN), trains on a large corpus (527,830 train articles) and evaluates on multiple datasets including a new KP20k testset, reporting substantial (>20%) gains on present-keyphrase extraction. The model design and training objective are technically sound and consistent with established sequence-to-sequence and copy mechanisms. Minor methodological gaps remain — comparisons are against traditional extractive baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui) with no mention of modern neural baselines or statistical significance testing — but these do not materially undermine the reported empirical improvements. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented experiments, with only small shortcomings in experimental breadth and reporting.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly supported by extensive experiments: a generative encoder-decoder (bi-GRU encoder, GRU decoder) with attention and a copy mechanism (CopyRNN) is evaluated on multiple standard datasets and a large new KP20k test set, showing >20% improvement over strong baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, Maui, etc.) on present keyphrase extraction. The methodological description (model architecture, training on a 527k+ corpus, beam search inference) and reported metrics (precision/recall/F1) make the experimental results credible. Minor technical concerns remain — no mention of statistical significance testing or code release for reproducibility is given — but these do not undermine the main experimental claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its CopyRNN against a broad set of established extractive and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank, KEA, Maui) across multiple standard datasets (Krapivin, Inspec, NUS, SemEval-2010, KP20k) using the same macro-averaged precision/recall/F1 metrics, and reports conditions such as ExpandRank failing on KP20k. Comparisons appear fair and differences (e.g., impact of the copy mechanism and time complexity issues) are analyzed, and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to other recent neural/generative sequence-to-sequence baselines beyond a vanilla RNN, nor clear acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably or better, so some minor omissions remain. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of standard baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank, KEA, Maui) across multiple benchmark datasets (Krapivin, Inspec, NUS, SemEval-2010, KP20k) using consistent metrics (macro P/R/F1). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors discuss limitations (e.g., ExpandRank timing out on KP20k, error examples for CopyRNN) and provide clear analysis of how their copy+attention variant differs from vanilla RNNs. Minor omissions remain possible (no mention of more recent neural keyphrase generation baselines), so I rate it as good comparisons with only small gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its CopyRNN against a broad set of established extractive and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank, KEA, Maui) across multiple standard datasets (Krapivin, Inspec, NUS, SemEval-2010, KP20k) using the same macro-averaged precision/recall/F1 metrics, and reports conditions such as ExpandRank failing on KP20k. Comparisons appear fair and differences (e.g., impact of the copy mechanism and time complexity issues) are analyzed, and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to other recent neural/generative sequence-to-sequence baselines beyond a vanilla RNN, nor clear acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably or better, so some minor omissions remain.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of standard baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank, KEA, Maui) across multiple benchmark datasets (Krapivin, Inspec, NUS, SemEval-2010, KP20k) using consistent metrics (macro P/R/F1). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors discuss limitations (e.g., ExpandRank timing out on KP20k, error examples for CopyRNN) and provide clear analysis of how their copy+attention variant differs from vanilla RNNs. Minor omissions remain possible (no mention of more recent neural keyphrase generation baselines), so I rate it as good comparisons with only small gaps.

main_claims: Propose a generative deep keyphrase generation model (encoder-decoder) named Deep Keyphrase Generation, which can capture deep semantic meaning of content.

Introduce a CopyRNN variant that incorporates a copy mechanism and attention, which significantly outperforms a vanilla RNN model and existing baselines on extracting present keyphrases (exceeding best baselines by more than 20% on average).
The proposed model can generate absent (not-present-in-text) topical keyphrases based on semantic understanding of the source text.
Construct and use a large training corpus of scientific metadata (567,830 articles total, trained on 527,830) and introduce a new large test set KP20k (20,000 articles).
_summary.methodology: Use an RNN encoder-decoder framework with a bidirectional GRU encoder, a (forward) GRU decoder, attention mechanism, and a copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to generate keyphrases from source text (title+abstract); models are trained jointly to maximize conditional probability and use beam search at inference.
datasets: Collected corpus of 567,830 scientific articles (trained on 527,830)
limitations: A pure RNN generative model tends to produce overly general keyphrases and fails to recall keyphrases with out-of-vocabulary words (vocabulary limited to top 50,000).
CopyRNN can still make mistaken predictions (example: wrongly predicting 'rich content' coordinated with 'video metadata').
ExpandRank fails to return any result on the KP20k dataset due to high time complexity.
The model trained on publication data was applied to news articles without adaptive training and may benefit from domain-specific training.
